calendar.matters:
MASTER CALENDAR JURY
Off Calendar Jan-15-2026 Continued To Jun-15-26 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

MOTION TO STRIKE Amended CROSS COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Sep-29-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. Gordon Renneisen, Esq. of Cornerstone Law Group (tel: 415-357-2094) for the Plaintiff.
Atty. Myles Lanzone, Esq. of Gordon Rees Scully Mansukhani, LLP (tel: 415-986-5900) for the Defendant.

Hearing Re: PLAINTIFF HOUSING AUTHORITY OF THE CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's MOTION TO STRIKE AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. Upon conclusion, the Court adopts its tentative ruling.

The Housing Authority of the City and County of San Francisco's motion to strike specified material from Nan McKay and Associates, Inc.'s first amended cross-complaint (FACC) is denied.

Code of Civil Procedure, section 436, subdivision (a), permits the court to strike "irrelevant, false, or improper matter inserted in any pleading." But because pleadings are liberally construed (id., section 452), courts exercise this power sparingly and generally strike material only that is blatantly false or irrelevant to the case at hand. (See PH II, Inc. v. Superior Court (1995) 33 Cal.App.4th 1680, 1683.) The court has reviewed each of the words and phrases that the Housing Authority seeks to strike and is satisfied that a motion to strike does not lie. The challenged material is only irrelevant or improper if the court adopts the Housing Authority's view of the contract between the parties, and the scope of the obligations imposed by the contract, which is contrary to the court's obligation to construe the pleading liberally.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the court's ruling; with a cover letter reflecting compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)

MASTER CALENDAR JURY
Off Calendar Feb-20-2025 Continued To Mar-16-26 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

CROSS DEFENDANT HOUSING AUTHORITY OF THE CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSES TO SPECIAL INTERROGATORIES (ADDED FOR POSTING OF PART 2 OF 2 OF THE TENTATIVE RULING).
Off Calendar Mar-04-2025 Off Calendar

Motion To Compel Further Responses To Special Interrogatories
Off Calendar Mar-04-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

CROSS DEFENDANT HOUSING AUTHORITY OF THE CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO Motion To Compel Further Responses To Special Interrogatories

Moving party Housing Authority of the City and County of San Francisco seeks an order compelling further responses to its Special Interrogatory Nos. 19 and 21.

Special Interrogatory 19 seeks "all facts in detail" that support a particular contention responding party Nan McKay & Associates, Inc. has made in the litigation. Nan McKay responded with objections and then provided two examples, described in general terms as a pattern rather than a description of specific occasions, of facts supporting its contention. The court orders Nan McKay to supplement its response. Its objections lack merit; a party is entitled through the use of interrogatories to learn the facts on which an opponent bases its legal contentions. (CCP 2030.010(b).) Nan McKay's use of two generalized examples, rather than all specific facts on which its contentions are based, does not comply with its obligation to provide "as complete and straightforward" a response "as the information reasonably available to the responding party permits." (Id. 2030.220(a).) If Nan McKay does not yet have full information to make a complete response (such as because it is awaiting documents in discovery), it must nonetheless answer fully to the best of its present ability. (Id. 2030.220(b).) Nan McKay's objection that the interrogatory mistakenly refers to its response to RFA 9 is unpersuasive; the Housing Authority quoted the contention for which it seeks Nan McKay's supporting facts and thus is intelligible.

Special Interrogatory 21 seeks a description of all "standard operating procedures" that Nan McKay claims the Housing Authority required it to exceed. Nan McKay responded by first referring to Contract No. 19-0014, contending that it identifies the procedures, and second by giving examples of standard procedures for HAP reconciliations that the Housing Authority asked it to exceed. The court finds that Nan McKay's invocation of 2030.230 is proper-it may refer to the contract in its response-but nonetheless incomplete. The question was not what SOPs applied to Nan McKay, which might be supplied by the contract, but which SOPs Nan McKay was asked by the Housing Authority to exceed. Unless the answer is "all of the SOPs contained in the contract," Nan McKay's reference to the contract's procedural requirements does not answer the question. Nan McKay shall supplement its response by specifying which procedural requirements of the contract it was asked or required by the Housing Authority to exceed.

Nan McKay shall provide supplemental answers within 15 days of entry of this order in light of the parties' impending trial date. No party seeks sanctions. The court shall nonetheless order sanctions payable by the unsuccessful party unless it finds that the unsuccessful party acted with substantial justification. The court finds that both parties acted with substantial justification and does not award sanctions.

Order is signed.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (301/CVA)
